channel.title: AP News 全文 RSS（简体中文版）
channel.description: AP News 首页抓取 + 英译简

item.title: 赫格斯在法国诺曼底登陆演讲中援引移民和“入侵”
item.description:
巴黎（美联社）——周六，美国国防部长皮特·赫格斯在诺曼底登陆周年纪念演讲中似乎将海上移民与欧洲战时解放联系起来，并警告称，如果领导人未能捍卫盟军赢得的自由，那么盟军赢得的自由可能只是暂时的。
赫格斯在法国西北部滨海科勒维尔的诺曼底美军公墓纪念 1944 年 6 月 6 日登陆 82 周年期间发表讲话，他说，今天，“不同的欧洲海滩受到不同危险意识形态的袭击。”
“西班牙、意大利、希腊和保加利亚的海滩。船只和人员抵达，”他说。
“欧洲各国政府什么时候才能对这次入侵采取行动？还是为时已晚？”他补充道。“我祈祷不会，我也不相信。”
赫格斯没有使用“移民”这个词，但他的言论呼应了特朗普政府在移民、边境以及美国官员所说的对民族主义和极右声音的审查方面对欧洲的更广泛的批评。
周六，英国首相凯尔·斯塔默办公室谴责美国副总统万斯将杀害南安普敦 18 岁英国学生亨利·诺瓦克的事件归咎于移民，尽管诺瓦克和凶手都是英国人。
去年12月，特朗普政府的国家安全战略警告称，欧洲面临“文明毁灭的前景”，并可能在20年内变得“面目全非”。

item.title: 牙买加罕见全岛停电后紧急恢复供电
item.description:
特立尼达西班牙港（美联社）——牙买加国家电力供应商表示，在停电导致整个岛屿整晚断电后，他们正在努力于周六早些时候恢复服务。
该国唯一的能源供应商牙买加公共服务公司证实，停电是在晚上 9 点刚过。周五，这个拥有 280 万人口的加勒比小国的所有公用事业客户都受到了影响。
该公司总裁休·格兰特表示，当局仍在调查停电原因，但很可能与袭击主要变电站和其他电网基础设施附近的闪电有关。
牙买加能源部​​长达里尔·瓦兹 (Daryl Vaz) 表示，截至周六上午，70 万用户中的约 50 万用户已恢复供电，预计数小时内即可完全恢复。
他在 X 上写道：“我致力于向全国通报这一不可接受的情况并通报最新情况。”
这种突然的全系统电网故障在牙买加很少见，那里的重大中断通常与恶劣天气有关。2025 年 10 月，梅丽莎飓风袭击该岛，导致数十万客户断电。

item.title: Spearfisher在澳大利亚4周内发生的第三起致命鲨鱼袭击事件中丧生
item.description:
澳大利亚墨尔本（美联社）——周六，一名鱼叉捕鱼者在澳大利亚四个星期内第三次致命的鲨鱼袭击中丧生，警方称。
警方声明称，这名 35 岁男子正与家人在西澳大利亚州港口城市奥尔巴尼附近的米迦勒马岛附近进行鱼叉捕鱼，中午前遭到袭击。
警方称，这名男子被乘船送往奥尔巴尼，医护人员正在等待，但他无法苏醒。当局怀疑是一条 4.5 米（15 英尺）长的白鲨造成的。
对于近几十年来平均每年大约有三起鲨鱼死亡的国家来说，连续发生三起鲨鱼死亡事件是非同寻常的。
最后一次死亡事件发生在 5 月 24 日，当时 39 岁的鱼叉捕鱼者 Michael Jensz 在澳大利亚东北海岸大堡礁的一次袭击中头部受伤，无法生还。附近有人看到牛鲨。
一周前的 5 月 16 日，一条 4 米（13 英尺）长的白鲨在罗特内斯特岛附近的奥尔巴尼西北部咬死了 38 岁的鱼叉渔民史蒂夫·马塔博尼 (Steve Mattabonni)。
澳大利亚今年唯一一起致命的鲨鱼袭击事件发生在 1 月份，一名 12 岁男孩在悉尼港被牛鲨咬伤几天后在医院死亡。

item.title: 俄亥俄州音乐节至少有 12 人遭枪击，警方称仍在搜寻嫌疑人
item.description:
周六，俄亥俄州一个繁忙的街头节日附近爆发了枪声，造成至少 12 人受伤，一些参加活动的人争先恐后地寻找掩护，而其他人则急忙帮助受害者。
托莱多副警察局长乔·赫弗南 (Joe Heffernan) 表示，几小时后没有嫌疑人被拘留，官员们敦促参加音乐节的人们提供手机上的照片或视频，以寻找可能的线索。
枪击事件发生在老西区音乐节附近，这是一年一度的现场音乐和家庭巡演聚会。
赫弗南表示，至少有两人开枪，“可能是在互相射击”。
赫弗南补充说，其中两名受害者情况危急。受害者年龄从14岁到61岁不等，其中大多数20岁出头。
“我对今晚托莱多的局势深感担忧，”俄亥俄州州长迈克·德温在一份声明中说。“夏季节日应该是家庭共度时光的安全场所，而不必担心暴力。”
社交媒体上发布的多段视频显示，人们在枪声中奔跑，应急官员正在照顾其他似乎受伤的人。
消防队长艾利森·阿姆斯特朗表示，由于道路封闭和人们离开节日的交通，很难到达医院，但紧急救援人员能够在一小时内将所有患者从现场转移出去。
凯文·贝里 (Kevin Berry) 正和朋友坐在附近的植物园里听现场音乐，突然听到几声枪响。
当贝里回头看时，他看到一把枪被扔在距离他不到 50 英尺（15 米）的地方。已经在现场参加节日的官员立即做出了回应。
贝里接受过医疗培训并在海军服役，他四处寻找可能需要帮助的人，发现至少有五人受枪伤。
“被击中的人分散在植物园周围，”他说。
老西区节是在托莱多历史街区举行的为期两天的庆祝活动，包括现场音乐、食品摊贩、家庭旅游和购物。贝里将其描述为“托莱多夏季节日季的开始”。
该市安全总监乔治·克拉尔(George Kral)表示，官员们正在与组织者讨论活动是否会持续到周末。
“这是托莱多最具标志性的节日之一，”他说，“很遗憾这样的事情不得不毁掉它。”

item.title: 关于新泽西州拘留中心外正在进行的抗议和逮捕活动，您需要了解什么
item.description:
自五月下旬以来，示威者与执法人员在新泽西州移民拘留中心外发生的冲突已成为抗议唐纳德·特朗普总统移民镇压行动的最新爆发点。
纽瓦克德莱尼厅发生一系列抗议活动后，已有 80 多人被捕，示威者表示，他们是为了声援被拘留者生活条件恶劣的指控。
特朗普政府为这座拥有 1000 个床位的设施中被拘留者的待遇进行了辩护。
该设施归 Geo Group 所有，该集团是一家私人监狱承包商，在全国各地经营多个拘留中心。
据移民倡导组织 Cosecha 称，被拘留者上个月发起了绝食抗议。他们发出了多封来自内部的手写信，表达了他们的要求，包括改善生活条件和更好的医疗服务，因为他们说有些人被拒绝接受药物治疗。
全国各地的被拘留者都声称患有严重和慢性疾病而受到医疗忽视，他们还宣布在其他设施进行绝食。
德莱尼厅的被拘留者表示，他们被给予发霉和过期的食物，其中一些食物还长有蛆虫，而且他们被关押在缺乏空调的拥挤牢房中。来自纽约市的民主党国会议员在抗议活动初期参观了该设施，并表示被拘留者的说法是可信的。
唐纳德·特朗普总统和他的副手为该中心的运作进行了辩护，并否认内部有任何绝食、虐待或条件恶劣的情况。
“事实是，我们正在给他们提供他们想要的卡路里，”国土安全部部长马克韦恩·穆林最近表示。“这不是假日酒店。”
特朗普的边境沙皇汤姆·霍曼在最近访问后称赞了这里的食物。“意大利面很好吃，”他告诉哥伦比亚广播公司新闻。
GEO 集团驳斥了这些批评，称其为“外部团体出于政治动机发起的一场运动，旨在通过针对政府设施承包商来拆除 ICE 和联邦移民拘留所。”
抗议者中的一些人戴着防毒面具和头盔，他们使用交通锥、垃圾桶和其他物品作为临时盾牌，并试图阻止车辆进出该设施。
美国代理司法部长托德·布兰奇在网上分享了美国移民和海关执法局官员受伤和瘀伤的照片。
社交媒体上的视频显示，身穿防暴装备的警察使用催泪瓦斯并使用警棍击退示威者。一些视频显示警察骑马冲进人群。
国土安全部部长马克韦恩·穆林 (Markwayne Mullin) 在 X 节目中表示，周五晚上，ICE 官员以袭击执法人员、妨碍执法和威胁等罪名逮捕了四人。与此同时，纽瓦克警方表示，一名西雅图男子因砸碎车窗而被指控犯有刑事恶作剧罪。
纽瓦克公共安全总监伊曼纽尔·米兰达（Emanuel Miranda）在社交媒体上发帖称：“任何人都无权进入我们的城市、破坏个人财产或煽动暴力。”“在带着和平抗议以外的任何其他议程来纽瓦克之前要三思而后行。”
一名执法官员被指控从一名在报道对峙过程中受伤的美联社摄影记者那里窃取了价值 10,000 美元的摄像设备。在警察与示威者的冲突中，记者安吉丽娜·卡萨尼斯的膝盖被木梁击中。该州总检察长周四表示，她寻求医疗救助，并使用地理跟踪设备追踪她丢失的装备，找到了他的家。
民主党州长米基·谢里尔最初不愿干预日益严重的抗议活动。但随着暴力事件加剧，她宣称局势“变得不安全”和“不可接受”，并呼吁州警察恢复秩序。
驻守在拘留中心前的 ICE 官员同意辞职，州警察设立了指定的抗议区和车辆检查站。
与此同时，纽瓦克市市长 Ras Baraka 下令晚上 9 点禁止通行。宵禁并派出市警察来执行。
但就在一周后，这位民主党人表示，该市将缩减警力，称逮捕人数有所下降，而且他不愿意继续花费税款来保卫私人设施。
该州总检察长本周对 Geo Group 提起诉讼，指控该机构拒绝州卫生检查员“完全进入”调查指控。

item.title: 法官驳回肯尼迪中心对取消平安夜演出的音乐家的诉讼
item.description:
音乐家查克·雷德 (Chuck Redd) 的律师表示，华盛顿特区高等法院法官驳回了针对雷德提起的违约诉讼，此前雷德取消了在肯尼迪中心的圣诞夜演出，以抗议唐纳德·特朗普总统对该场馆的影响力。
周五的解雇是根据华盛顿的反 SLAPP 法律批准的，该法律旨在防止旨在压制公共利益问题上的反对观点的毫无根据的诉讼。
雷德是一名鼓手和电颤琴演奏家，曾与迪兹·吉莱斯皮 (Dizzy Gillespie) 和雷·布朗 (Ray Brown) 等所有人一起巡演。自 2006 年以来，他一直在肯尼迪中心主持节日“爵士乐演奏会”。在特朗普精心挑选的肯尼迪中心董事会投票决定将总统的名字加入该设施后不久，他取消了去年的演出。
“该中心起诉雷德先生，因为他公开且正确地反对将唐纳德·特朗普的名字添加到肯尼迪中心，这是前总统约翰·F·肯尼迪的活生生的纪念碑，”雷德的律师之一丽莎·J·班克斯在一份声明中说。“针对雷德先生的诉讼纯粹是特朗普肯尼迪中心的政治报复，法院正确地认为这是出于偏见而驳回此案。”
雷德在周六早上的一封电子邮件中告诉美联社，他“对法官的裁决非常满意”。
驳回动议于三月提交，认为雷德没有合同义务履行义务。其中包括肯尼迪中心提供的合同，但艺术家从未签署过。
肯尼迪中心的代表没有立即回应就该诉讼被驳回发表评论的请求。

item.title: 特朗普赦免被判内幕交易罪的前共和党国会议员
item.description:
华盛顿（美联社）——唐纳德·特朗普总统赦免了印第安纳州前共和党国会议员斯蒂芬·买家，他在卸任后因利用内幕信息进行非法股票交易而被判入狱近两年。
买方在 2023 年担任顾问和说客时因交易被判处 22 个月监禁。他被责令没收违法所得35万多美元，并罚款1万美元。他于 2025 年获释。
最高法院于 5 月驳回了买方的上诉，未发表评论或提出异议。
特朗普在授予“全面、彻底、无条件的赦免”时，提到了买尔在陆军和众议院担任军法署署长的职业生涯“杰出且富有成效”。赦免日期为周四，并于周五晚间由白宫发布。
买家表示，这次赦免“纠正了出于政治动机的起诉”，并且“因为我没有犯下的罪行而被监禁，这是可怕的”。他坚称自己是无辜的。
2022 年 7 月 27 日，美国前众议员史蒂芬·拜尔 (Stephen Buyer) 在纽约担任顾问时参与了内幕交易计划，随后他对指控表示无罪，随后离开曼哈顿联邦法院。（美联社照片/拉里·纽迈斯特，档案）
特朗普于 5 月 31 日利用其“真相”社交媒体平台分享了两封请求总统赦免拜尔的信件。拜尔是一名律师，也是 2011 年离任的海湾战争老兵。他曾是民主党总统比尔·克林顿 1998 年弹劾审判的众议院检察官，并于 2016 年在特朗普的过渡团队中任职，专注于退伍军人问题。
一封由国会 40 多名前共和党议员签署的信称，拜尔因参与克林顿的审判而成为“深层政府的目标”。
他们在 2025 年 4 月的信中写道：“总统先生，像您一样，史蒂夫也是拜登政府执法的受害者。”
2026 年 6 月 5 日星期五，美国国会大厦位于华盛顿。（美联社照片/玛丽亚姆·祖海布）
五名现任众议院共和党议员的第二封信表示，赦免买家将为他的案件带来正义。这封 2025 年 6 月的信函由俄克拉荷马州的汤姆·科尔、加利福尼亚州的肯·卡尔弗特、印第安纳州的马林·斯塔茨曼、密歇根州的杰克·伯格曼和德克萨斯州的皮特·塞辛斯签署。
67 岁的买家因涉及 2018 年 4 月宣布的 T-Mobile 和 Sprint 265 亿美元合并的内幕交易以及管理咨询公司 Navigant 的非法交易而被定罪，当时他的客户 Guidehouse 准备在几周后公开披露的交易中收购该公司。
宪法赋予总统广泛的权力来赦免联邦罪行。赦免不会消除接受者的犯罪记录，但可以被视为仁慈或正义的行为。

item.title: 女海军军官表示，赫格斯将女性从晋升名单中剔除后，她们担心职业生涯会受到限制
item.description:
WASHINGTON (AP) — AfterDefense Secretary Pete Hegsethcut nine Navy officers, including all the women, from a promotion list, several female officers say they see the unusual intervention as a sign that their careers now have a ceiling and worry for thefuture generation of female military leaders.
The Navy had selected 31 sailors to promote from the rank of captain to one-star admiral, but Hegseth recently intervened to strike nine people from the list, including three women and two Black men, according to a defense official who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss information not permitted to be released publicly.
As a result, the Navy is not promoting a single woman to the one-star admiral rank this year even though women make up about one-quarter of all Navy officers and nearly one-third of the sea service’s midgrade ranks, according to military data from 2024.
The Associated Press spoke with eight female Navy officers of varying ranks and time in service after Hegseth’s cuts, which were reported earlier by The New York Times, became public. They spoke on condition of anonymity out of fear of retribution from their superiors.
The more junior officers said they saw the development as a sign that their careers would become politicized if they rose too far in the ranks, and some said they felt they now had a limit on how far they could be promoted. Some said it made them feel less valued within the military and wondered whether that wasn’t part of the intent.
The Pentagon has not offered any rationale on why the women, or any of the other six people, were removed from the promotion list.
The Pentagon is viewed from the window of an airplane Aug. 27, 2023, in Washington. (AP Photo/Carolyn Kaster, File)
Sean Parnell, the Pentagon’s top spokesman, said on social media this week that “military promotions are given to those who have earned them” and that the Pentagon “will never consider the color of a service member’s skin or their gender as a factor in promotions.” The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request seeking further comment.
The Navy’s process for choosing which officers to promote to the one-star rank has been relatively constant and transparent over the years. The service convenes a group of officers, called a promotion board, that examines the records of eligible officers and chooses the most qualified.
The board that selected the initial slate of 31 officers for promotion was directed by then-Navy Secretary John Phelan, an appointee of President Donald Trump, to “recommend for promotion the best qualified officers within their respective competitive category.”
The order from Phelan, who laterabruptly departed his postin April, said the board should consider an officer’s performance, competence and character, among other traits, as part of those qualifications.
Secretary of the Navy John Phelan listens as President Donald Trump speaks at his Mar-a-Lago club, Dec. 22, 2025, in Palm Beach, Fla. (AP Photo/Alex Brandon, File)
It also said that given China’s prominence in theTrump administration’s National Defense Strategy, “special consideration shall be given to officers who have excelled in their knowledge of the political military affairs and U.S. strategic interests in the Indo-Pacific region, and operational contingency planning for Indo-Pacific war plans.”
Hegseth has long argued,without offering evidence, that women in the military benefit from preferential treatment and arenot suited for combat roles.
“For too long, we’ve promoted too many uniformed leaders for the wrong reasons based on their race, based on gender quotas, based on historic so-called firsts,”Hegseth told hundreds of military leadersin September.
The approach, he asserted, made the Pentagon “less capable and less lethal.”
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Dan Caine arrive for a Senate Appropriations subcommittee on defense hearing on the budget request for the Department of Defense, Tuesday, May 12, 2026, in Washington. (AP Photo/Alex Brandon)
Phelan’s order said the Navy cannot discriminate based on criteria such as race and sex, and it specifically noted that “this guidance shall not be interpreted as requiring or permitting preferential treatment of any officer or group of officers on the grounds of race, religion, color, sex.”
The full list of 31 people to be promoted was approved by Phelan, other Navy leaders and the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Gen. Dan Caine, before it reached Hegseth, who chose to make the changes, the defense official said.
While Hegseth is within his rights to intervene in the list, “it’s just not the norm” and its “a break from tradition” said Katherine Kuzminski, a researcher specializing in military recruiting and retention at the Center for New American Security think tank. She said that promotions historically have been seen “the services’ business.”
Kuzminski noted that “this is a decision that’s not being made by the U.S. Navy — it’s being made by the secretary of defense” and said Hegseth’s growing interference in operational aspects of the military services such as promotions is creating “tension” about what “normal” will look like going forward.
Some of the more senior Navy officers who spoke with the AP expressed concerns about the message it sends to the next generation of young sailors.
Naval Academy students enter Navy Marine Corps Stadium during the U.S. Naval Academy’s graduation and commissioning ceremony at the U.S. Naval Academy, May 22, 2026, in Annapolis, Md. (AP Photo/Terrance Williams, File)
In addition to pulling the recent promotions of three women to admiral, Hegseth shortly after he took officefired Adm. Lisa Franchetti, the service’s top officer and the first woman to hold the job. He never explained his rationale.
Since then, he also has fired two other female three-star admirals without explanation.
Some of the officers who spoke to the AP said that while they were encouraging female sailors to stick with the Navy, they acknowledged that message is coming at a difficult time.
Kuzminski said the rhetoric and actions surrounding women in the military “affects individual service member decision-making and it also affects family unit decision-making,” including whether people make a career of the military.
Kuzminski said that following the monthslonghold on military promotionsby Sen. Tommy Tuberville, R-Ala.,during the Biden administration, surveys showed that partisan politics spilling into the day-to-day lives of troops affected their decision-making.
One officer said this impact was not confined to women.
In conversations with other sailors in her unit, she said that male sailors were hesitant to deal with what appears to be a growing politicization of simply following the orders of previous administrations.

item.title: Golden Tempo 在赢得肯塔基德比赛后赢得贝尔蒙特锦标赛第 158 届冠军
item.description:
纽约州萨拉托加斯普林斯（美联社）——“黄金节奏”准确地展示了为什么他是一个出色的终结者，周六他在贝尔蒙特锦标赛上的伸展跑为训练师切丽·德沃创造了更多的历史。
在骑师何塞·奥尔蒂斯 (Jose Ortiz) 的策骑下，黄金节奏 (Golden Tempo) 从领先者落后 12 个马位的位置上脱颖而出，赢得了比赛的第 158 场比赛。这场胜利是在他最后一次赢得肯塔基德比比赛五周后取得的。
“黄金节奏太棒了。何塞太棒了，”德沃说。“我认为他需要这样做，以表明他注定要赢得德比，并且他是一匹属于顶级三岁马之一的马。”
Golden Tempo 以 6-1 的赔率，以一码又四分之一的优势击败 Commandment。《戒律》位居第二，最受欢迎的《叛徒》位居第三。
“我在第二回合就紧紧跟随他们，”奥尔蒂斯说。“他们开始捡起它，我也这么做了。我只是在等待合适的时机全力以赴。当我要求他走时，我的马回应了。”
德沃成为第一位训练肯塔基德比赛冠军的女性，也是四年来第二位在贝尔蒙特做到这一点的女性。耶娜·安东努奇 (Jena Antonucci) 于 2023 年与阿尔坎杰洛 (Arcangelo) 一起赢得了肯塔基德比冠军。德沃是第一位赢得多项三冠王比赛的女性。
“这太令人难以承受了，”德沃说。“所有功劳都归功于赢得比赛的 Golden Tempo，而何塞在实现这一目标方面做得非常出色。但我很幸运能够担任这个职位。这是创造历史，我有点回避它，但我真的很感激我就是那个人。”
德沃出生于萨拉托加温泉，并在那里开始了她的训练生涯，但她并不称自己是萨拉托加本地人。她现在大部分时间都在肯塔基州度过，并说她在佛罗里达州南部长大。但在得到了镇上的所有支持后，她开始接受它。
“每个人都称我为家乡女孩，”德沃说。“所以这很有趣。我会想念萨拉托加的开胃菜，我们称之为‘Bellatoga’。”
Golden Tempo 是比赛中的第三选择。人们担心他是否能在包括叛逆者在内的领域再次取得大逆转胜利。这匹经过托德·普莱彻 (Todd Pletcher) 训练的马在德比大赛中以微弱优势落后于 Golden Tempo，获得第二名。
虽然速度远不如丘吉尔唐斯那么快，但 Golden Tempo 仍然能够及时接近，以 2 分 03.49 秒的成绩赢得了 1 1/4 英里的比赛。但这并不重要，因为他是九匹马中最好的。
“他不会像在德比中那样获得那样的设置，”奥尔蒂斯说。“我们都知道这一点，我有点担心。他需要某种安排。但今天没有，但他今天出现并赢了。”
在德沃和业主决定绕过普里克内斯之后，黄金节奏赢得了三冠王的三分之二。他是多年来第二匹在没有参加中间宝石比赛后赢得肯塔基德比赛和贝尔蒙特赛的马。
“我们做出了决定，今天我们赢了，我们会为此感到高兴，”德沃说。
这是贝尔蒙塔萨拉托盖恩纽约州北部第三次也是最后一次，而其位于皇后区和长岛边境的传统住宅正在被拆除和重建。由于萨拉托加赛道的赛程为 1 1/4 英里，比赛将于明年回到贝尔蒙特公园，届时将恢复传统的 1 1/2 英里距离。
“这非常有意义，”德沃说。“这里有很多家庭。萨拉托加，在这里举办如此历史性的比赛真是太棒了……这非常有意义，因为这个小镇可以拥有这样的赛事并与我们一起庆祝。”
Golden Tempo 为获胜支付了 14 美元，为名次支付了 7.32 美元，为出场费支付了 3.88 美元。Commandment 支付了 7.02 美元的出场费用和 4.08 美元的排名费用，而 Renegade 支付了 2.52 美元的排名费用。
奥尔蒂斯跟随由哥哥伊拉德 (Irad) 驾驶的叛徒 (Renegade)，就像他在德比中所做的那样。六月的第一个星期六和五月的第一个星期六的结果是一样的。
“他今天有点弹跳，这让我非常高兴，因为我希望他今天变得更敏锐一点，”何塞·奥尔蒂斯说。“你可以看到他，他非常放松。他按照我的要求去做。这是最重要的。”
共同所有者维尼·维奥拉 (Vinnie Viola) 将这场比赛献给了他已故的朋友多米尼克·迪普里斯科 (Dominic DiPrisco)，后者于周三去世，享年 70 岁。维奥拉周六早上向迪普里斯科祈祷，希望能在贝尔蒙特锦标赛中获得额外的推动力。
“我知道你在天堂，我爱你，这场比赛是为了你，”维奥拉说。“这意味着我现在无法用言语表达。”
奥尔蒂斯 (Ortiz) 第二次赢得贝尔蒙特锦标赛 (Belmont Stakes)，距 2017 年在 Tapwrit 首次夺冠九年后。
“我们只是希望他变得更好并继续赢得此类比赛，”奥尔蒂斯说。“我们对他感到非常满意。这都是关于他的。”
纽约美联社体育记者斯蒂芬·怀诺 (Stephen Whyno) 对此作出了贡献。
美联社赛马：https://apnews.com/hub/horse-racing

item.title: 俄罗斯少女米拉·安德烈娃表示，她必须克服内心的恶魔才能赢得法网冠军
item.description:
PARIS (AP) — Bent over with her hands covering her face, her knees getting dirtied on the red clay court,Mirra Andreevawas celebrating — processing might be the more appropriate word — how she had finally overcome “so many demons inside” that came with being a teenage tennis phenom.
After bursting onto the scene at 15, Andreeva became a Grand Slam champion at 19 when the Russian ended the run of 114th-ranked Polish qualifierMaja Chwalinskawith a 6-3, 6-2 victory in theFrench Openfinal on Saturday.
“I’ve done a lot of visualizations before. Not just this tournament, but I’ve had dreams, I’ve had a lot of thoughts on how it’s going to happen, if it’s going to happen, when it’s going to happen, where,” Andreeva said, still hardly breathing as she talked quickly in true teenage style. “The feeling in real life is so much better than in your dreams.
“I can call myself a Grand Slam champion,” Andreeva added.
The biggest challenges for Andreeva have not been on the court — she already has one of the best attacking baseline games in the sport — it’s been the mental side. And her stubbornness.
Winner Russia’s Mirra Andreeva, left, and second placed Poland’s Maja Chwalinska pose with trophies after the final tennis match at the French Open in Paris, Saturday, June 6, 2026. (AP Photo/Thibault Camus)
“Her attitude is difficult,” said Conchita Martinez, Andreeva’s coach and a former Wimbledon champion. “You tell her something, and maybe she’s not open to listening. ... When she works hard and when she listens and she does everything, she has no limits.”
Andreeva acknowledged as much during the trophy ceremony.
“I know I can be a tough cookie sometimes and it’s pretty hard to put up with me,” Andreeva said.
The victory took Andreeva one step further than Martinez, who lost the 2000 French Open final to Mary Pierce.
Pierce presented the winner’s trophy to Andreeva, who became the youngest woman to win the clay-court Grand Slam since Monica Seles was 18 when she claimed her third straight French Open in 1992.
“You’re so young and talented. It’s so annoying,” the 24-year-old Chwalinska told Andreeva.
Andreeva took the unusual step of thanking herself “for believing in myself, always giving my 100%, even when it’s tough, trying every day to be better as a person and as a player, believing that I can do this, fighting so many demons inside of me.
“Only I know how tough it was for me,” Andreeva added. “How nervous I was throughout these two weeks.”
Russia’s Mirra Andreeva ewacts after winning the final tennis match against Poland’s Maja Chwalinska at the French Open in Paris, Saturday, June 6, 2026. (AP Photo/Thibault Camus)
Andreeva also thanked her psychologist, who she said was watching from Florida: “Everything that you’ve told me I’ve been trying to use these two weeks.”
Chwalinska was attempting to become the first qualifier to capture the Roland Garros title. She was a promising junior alongside four-time Roland Garros champion Iga Swiatek before she began struggling with depression in 2019.
“Tennis is such a tough sport. It’s so individual. We start so early. We are basically kids when we start,” Chwalinska said. “People are expecting that we are going to behave like adults already and we are just kids really. So the pressure is huge.”
Andreeva was born in Siberia and moved to Sochi and eventually France to develop her tennis career.
She drew a loud applause from the crowd on Court Philippe-Chatrier when she spoke a few words of French during the trophy presentation.
“Thanks for your support today and over these past two marvelous weeks here in Paris,” Andreeva said in French. “It was very important for me.”
Russia’s Mirra Andreeva reacts after winning the semifinal tennis match against Ukraine’s Marta Kostyuk at the French Open in Paris, Thursday, June 4, 2026. (AP Photo/Thibault Camus)
Andreeva has been considered a Grand Slam contender since she burst onto the scene as a 15-year-old at the 2023 Madrid Open, where she became the third-youngest player to win a main draw match at a WTA 1000 tournament and made the quarterfinals.
Lately, Andreeva has had to contend with playing under neutral status and without her country’s flag because of the war with Ukraine.
When she beat Marta Kostyuk in the semifinals, Kostyuk refused to shake her hand, as has been the custom for Ukrainian players facing Russians ever since the war started in 2022.
“Every person doesn’t want to have a war in the world,” Andreeva said. “I never think about those things when I play.”
The final was played under a mostly sunny sky, though wind was a factor in the first Grand Slam final for both players.
Chwalinska double-faulted on the opening point of the match, but she was the first player to hold serve in the fifth game for a 3-2 lead.
But then Andreeva won nine stright games to take control as she found a way to hit through the wind and answer Chwalinska’s array of spins and drop shots.
Whereas Chwalinska would retreat to handle high balls in the wind, Andreeva often would move forward and take balls on the rise.
“She definitely handled wind much better than me,” Chwalinska said. “She was not running away from the ball.”
Andreeva produced 25 winners to Chwalinska’s 10 and also had fewer unforced errors: 26 to 29.
There was a strong Polish presence in the crowd.
When Chwalinska was introduced, fans held aloft red-and-white Polish flags and chanted her name: “Ma-ja, Ma-ja.”
Andreeva had little support from the crowd, although there was a shout of “Davai, Mirra!” (“Go, Mirra”) in Russian late in the match.
Alexander ZverevplaysFlavio Cobolliin the men’s final on Sunday to concludethe wildest Grand Slamin recent memory.
Russia’s Mirra Andreeva poses with the trophy after winning the final women’s tennis match against Poland’s Maja Chwalinska at the French Open in Paris, Saturday, June 6, 2026. (AP Photo/Aurelien Morissard)
AP Sports Writer Samuel Petrequin contributed to this report.

item.title: 世界杯在这个令人担忧的时刻提出了前所未有的安全挑战。美国准备好了吗？
item.description:
NEW YORK (AP) — TheWorld Cup,a 48-team, 104-match behemoth kicking off next week across 16 cities in the United States, Mexico and Canada, presents an unprecedented security challenge, with more countries, games and alarger footprintthan ever before.
It also comes against the backdrop of the U.S. and Israel’swar with Iran, mounting political violence inPresident Donald Trump’s orbitand growing fears of artificial intelligence-fueled disruptions, creating a complex threat environment for authorities.
Overseeing the sprawling security apparatus is a legion of federal agencies, state and local police departments and private entities. Their responsibilities range from securing stadiums and fan zones to escorting teams and protecting dignitaries.
Their tools include hunter drones that can shoot nets over objects in restricted airspace, bag-inspecting robot dogs, giant X-ray trucks and thousands of AI-powered cameras trained on public spaces soon to be thronged by fans.
In the U.S., it’s “78 Super Bowls over 39 days,” said Andrew Giuliani, executive director of Trump’s World Cup task force, which is overseeing the multiagency effort.
“There’s never been a summer like this in American history from a security angle,” said Giuliani, son of former New York City Mayor Rudy Giuliani.
Andrew Giuliani, right, head of the White House FIFA World Cup task force, speaks at a news briefing about World Cup security, Thursday, June 4, 2026, in Miami. (AP Photo/Rebecca Blackwell)
The tournament has the same high-level federal security designation as the Super Bowl, just below a presidential inauguration or a national political convention, ensuring federal, state and local coordination. It coincides with other major events linked to the250th anniversary of America’s founding.
So far, Giuliani said, there are no credible threats.
The Department of Homeland Security, focused on Trump’s immigration enforcement crackdown and hit by a funding lapse only recently resolved, estimates up 7 million people will visit the U.S. for the World Cup.
The U.S. Secret Service, under scrutiny after security breaches and attempts on Trump’s life, is in charge of protecting world leaders who show up to cheer on their countries. Trump has expressed interest in attending a match.
“I feel very comfortable where we’re at, and we feel like we have a zero-fail mission,” Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told Congress this week, noting that the Secret Service was understaffed by about 860 agents. “But it’s going to be complicated.”
Officials have indicated they are confident they can keep Trump safe because they will be integrating his usual security into the robust World Cup plan on days he may watch a match.
The FBI has spent two years developing its security plan, incorporating lessons from other major events such as the Macy’s Thanksgiving Day Parade and New Year’s Eve ball drop in New York and testing them at smaller ones, including last week’s Israel Day parade in the city.
“We prepare for the worst day,” FBI Special Agent in Charge Amit Kachhia-Patel in New York told The Associated Press. “And that’s how we go into any single event.”
FBI Special Agent in Charge Amit Kachhia-Patel, Mission Services Division, walks through a portion of the agency’s Joint Operations Center in New York, Thursday, June 4, 2026, ahead of the World Cup soccer tournament. (AP Photo/Richard Drew)
To help cover security costs, the Federal Emergency Management Agency has distributed $625 million to the 11 U.S. host cities. An additional $250 million is being directed toward tracking and neutralizing suspect drones.
The disbursement of those funds was held up by the department’s funding delay, which the Republican administration has argued hindered security planning.
Others involved in the planning effort said the federal government could have played a more hands-on role even before the shutdown.
John Cohen, a former senior DHS official who has been briefing state leaders before the matches, said the government was largely absent from planning meetings last year and did not begin sharing threat intelligence with host regions until recently.
“With an event of this magnitude, one would expect the federal government would’ve played a more active role,” Cohen said. “It felt like a missed opportunity to showcase that collaboration.”
The World Cup, a 48-team, 104-match behemoth kicking off next week in 16 cities in the U.S., Mexico and Canada, presents an unprecedented security challenge. The FBI has been working on its tournament security plan for two years. (AP video by Joseph B. Frederick)
In January, thousands of officials involved in World Cup security gathered for exercises simulating crowd surges, vehicle attacks and mass shootings.
A month later, the U.S. and Israel launched a war with Iran.
“The security picture fundamentally changed,” said Stefano Ritondale, chief intelligence officer at Artorias, a defense intelligence company not involved in the security preparations. “There’s a major difference in preparing for a lone wolf radical who rams his car into a public place and a terrorist who is bankrolled by a foreign country we’re at war with.”
Since the last World Cup in Qatar in 2022, drones have become a prominent weapon in conflicts including Russia’s war in Ukraine and Hamas’ attack on Israel on Oct. 7, 2023.
“If there is one threat that keeps me up at night, it is from drones,” said New York City Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch, whose department is partnering with the FBI on drone mitigation.
Drones are prohibited over stadiums and fan zones, and Kachhia-Patel said the FBI has a “full suite of options” to thwart incursions. They include agents monitoring the sky and a “variety of means” to safely down the devices, he said without elaborating.
Before this year’s World Cup, the growing sophistication of AI videos was a particular concern, with officials warning that state actors can harness the technology to sow misinformation and panic.
On match days, the FBI will activate joint operations centers in each host city, bringing together local, state and federal law enforcement agencies to monitor and investigate threats.
“If there’s a video that shows an explosion going off at a site, and it’s AI generated, we have people on the ground who can validate whether or not that’s true,” said Kachhia-Patel.
A sign reading “FIFA World Cup 2026" is displayed at NYNJ Stadium, May 7, 2026, in East Rutherford, N.J. (AP Photo/Yuki Iwamura, File)
Some AI companies have pitched themselves to police departments in host cities, promising to comb through data and surveillance on game days to prevent threats, including unruly fan behavior.
“We know sports fanaticism around here in terms of the NFL and baseball to some extent, but nothing like international soccer,” said Jake Becchina, a police spokesperson in Kansas City, Missouri, which is hosting six matches.
The department has contracted with Peregrine Technologies, which promises to sift through police data and publicly available information such as team practice locations and the country affiliation of popular bars, to get ahead of possible conflict.
In Dallas, a recent $120 million tech upgrade will give local police body cameras capable of real-time translations, helping law enforcement communicate with international visitors soon to descend on the region.
Several drone detection and mitigation companies are joining efforts to help federal agencies secure the skies.
One of those companies, Fortem, has claimed to have signed a multimillion-dollar contract with DHS before the World Cup for an unusual drone mitigation strategy: quadcopters that can shoot nets at encroaching drones to trap them in midair. A spokesperson for DHS declined to discuss the contract.
Just as the teams will aim to perform their best on the pitch, Giuliani said the security planning was a unique chance to “show off American exceptionalism.”
“If we do our job right,” Giuliani added, “nobody will be talking about security at the World Cup.”
Santana reported from Washington. Associated Press writer Alanna Durkin Richer in Washington contributed to this report.

item.title: 英国高级王室成员齐聚一堂，查尔斯国王的侄子与护士哈里特·斯珀林结婚
item.description:
国王查尔斯三世和他的妻子卡米拉女王是英国王室的高级成员，他们戴着优雅的帽子、燕尾服和礼服，参加了周六查尔斯侄子彼得·菲利普斯的婚礼。
查尔斯妹妹安妮公主的儿子菲利普斯在英国西南部村庄肯布尔举行的教堂仪式上与为国家卫生服务中心工作的护士哈里特·斯珀林结婚。
包括威廉王子、凯瑟琳公主、尤金妮公主和比阿特丽斯公主以及其他王室成员在内的 100 多名嘉宾与他们一起出席。
新娘身穿艾米莉亚·威克斯泰德 (Emilia Wickstead) 设计的高领蕾丝礼服。
菲利普斯，48 岁，是安妮和她的第一任丈夫马克·菲利普斯的儿子。他是已故女王伊丽莎白二世的长孙，也是威廉和哈里王子的堂弟。
菲利普斯与第一任妻子奥特姆·凯利离婚，并育有两个孩子。他于去年宣布与斯珀林订婚。
当新娘和王室成员抵达时，祝福者欢呼雀跃，仪式结束后，宾客们在大雨中撑着雨伞，在这对夫妇离开教堂时向他们撒上玫瑰花瓣。

item.title: 《拉格泰姆》在百老汇首演 30 年后仍然引起观众共鸣
item.description:
New York (AP) — It’s been nearly 30 years since Stephen Flaherty and Lynn Ahrens wrote the music and lyrics for the musical“Ragtime,”an American epic tracking the intertwining lives of three families in New York at the turn of the 20th century.
Staged at Lincoln Center’s Vivian Beaumont Theater, the musical is in its third run on Broadway — and earned 11 Tony nominations, including for best revival. It’s resonating the most with audiences this time, they said. “Three is the charm,” Ahrens said.
“When we originally did it on Broadway, which was 1998, I think a lot of people, if not most people, were thinking about this piece as a period piece,” Flaherty said. “I think now, people are responding to it as a contemporary story.”
Adapted from the 1975 novel by E.L. Doctorow, the show’s book is by the lateplaywright Terrence McNally. It depicts a wide swath of the American experience in New York at the turn of the 20th century, from Black Americans in Harlem to Jewish immigrants on the Lower East Side to the white upper-class residents of the suburbs of Westchester County.
The story that unfolds is fiction, but features historical figures like activist Emma Goldman, educator and leader Booker T. Washington, banker J.P. Morgan, auto founder Henry Ford and illusionist Harry Houdini. The show’s breadth — encompassing immense tragedy as well as great optimism — and the depth of the actors’ performances has been bringing Broadway audiences to their feet, often mid-act.
Joshua Henry, foreground from left, Caissie Levy, and Brandon Uranowitz appear during a performance of “Ragtime” in New York on Oct. 8, 2025. (Matthew Murphy via AP)
It also has people returning. “They’re like, ‘I’m coming back with my parents,’ ‘I’m coming back with my grandchildren,’ ‘I’m coming back with my grandparents,’ and it’s not even like they have to see it. They want to experience it with them,” said Brandon Uranowitz, who had his own return to the show, decades after he acted as a child in the pre-Broadway production.
Now, he’s nominated for best lead actor in a musical for playing the role of Tateh, a Jewish immigrant from Latvia. “I think it’s sort of speaking to this generational reckoning that we’re having with America and our national identity.”
The original production lost the best new musicalTony Awardto“The Lion King,”but Ahrens and Flaherty took home the prize for best original score, McNally best book and William David Brohn best orchestrations in a competitive year. It also won Audra McDonald,the Tonys’ most decoratedperformer, her first award. A 2009 revival received six nominations, but lost best revival to “La Cage aux Folles.”
This could be the year it finally wins a best show award: “Ragtime” is a front-runner for best musical revival, against strong competition from“Cats: The Jellicle Ball”and “The Rocky Horror Show.” Among its other nods are nominations for all three leads, and for featured performersNichelle Lewisand Ben Levi Ross.
Joshua Henry,nominated alongside his costar for best lead actor, plays Coalhouse Walker Jr., a celebrated Black pianist at the center of his community in Harlem. Caissie Levy, nominated for her role of Mother, is the matriarch of a wealthy white family in a suburb outside New York City.
A cast of supporting characters, and a large ensemble, flesh out the lead trio’s lives, relationships and eventual connections: Lewis plays Sarah, Coalhouse’s beloved; Ross is Mother’s Younger Brother and Colin Donnell her husband, Father; Shaina Taub is Goldman, the real-life activist.
Emotions in the first act peak during “Wheels of a Dream,” Lewis’ iconic duet with Henry, which draws standing ovations, mid-song, nearly every night.
“She is a person who represents women — especially women of color — who don’t have a voice, women of color who are fighting to have a voice, women of color who find strength in other ways because we weren’t allowed to have it,” Lewis said of Sarah. But above all, she said, the character represents the power of trust, love and hope as a buoying force.
Nichelle Lewis, left, and Joshua Henry appear during a performance of “Ragtime” in New York on Oct. 8, 2025. (Matthew Murphy via AP)
That hope is also what propels Uranowitz’s Tateh. “Despite everything he goes through, despite the rejection, despite the oppression, despite the othering, despite antisemitism,” it’s what persists.
In the song “Journey On,” his character arrives in New York with his young daughter just as Mother’s husband, Father, leaves on an expedition to the North Pole.
“You depart on a ship from a country like this,” Tateh sings, watching Father leave. “Why on Earth would you want to be leaving?” The two men are perched on separate, moving staircases on a sparsely furnished stage, but sing from the same height, emphasizing the valley between their experiences.
Like many of the characters, Father and Tateh (also “father,” in Yiddish) are nameless. The intention, Uranowitz said, is for aspects of Tateh’s journey — from immigrant artist to successful moviemaker — to reflect the experience of Jewish Americans, and to resonate with people from other backgrounds as well. “If you pan out, which ‘Ragtime’ does so beautifully, it also holds just a capital ‘I’ immigration experience. And I think that’s really important for people to see right now.”
Brandon Uranowitz appears during a performance of “Ragtime” in New York on Oct. 8, 2025. (Matthew Murphy via AP)
The musical feels so relevant to 2026 that audience members have asked director Lear deBessonet, also Tony-nominated, if the creative team rewrote the script for this production. Lyrics by Ahrens and dialogue by McNally about the discrimination and brutality that Black Americans and immigrants face can seem straight out of the current moment. There are also references to keeping the country “great,” and commentary on celebrity culture and the power of industry leaders.
But the text hasn’t changed. “We, in the audience, are hearing it differently,” deBessonet said. “There’s something that actually, I think is very unifying about coming together with a community of our time to look at this other time, and to look at the promise and the wound of America right next to each other.”
A 2027 tour, with deBessonet and the Broadway run’s creative team at the helm, will bring the show to a wider audience around the country.
“It does not feel like we’re looking back. It feels like we’re looking in a mirror at ourselves,” Flaherty said.
There is one small change, however. When “Wheels of a Dream” is reprised in the final number, the ensemble sings “Our son will ride on the wheels of a,” and then takes a long pause before a final, resonant, “dream.” The goal is not to prescribe a particular emotional response, but to allow viewers — and the actors themselves — space for their own interpretations.
“In that moment, every single actor, every artist on that stage is invited to fill that moment with whatever feels honest to them that night,” deBessonet said. “Sometimes you can really feel that there is exuberant hope in the air. And sometimes there is grief or rage or confusion,” she added.
Ahrens said it’s been a “revelation” to see how audiences have responded to the production, before and after the 2024 presidential election — and throughout this Broadway run, which concludes on Aug. 2. “It’s such a visceral thing,” she said. “I don’t think we’ve ever experienced anything like it.”

item.title: 吸血鬼、文学怪物和蛋糕：托尼奖提供了许多有趣的、可能的奖项
item.description:
纽约（美联社）——飞行的吸血鬼。对热门电影《泰坦尼克号》的音乐恶搞。又一部恶搞，这次是百老汇黄金时代音乐剧。还有一部新的《推销员之死》，这是美国最华丽、最悲伤的戏剧之一。现在是托尼奖颁奖典礼的时间。
周日，24 场百老汇演出将希望在 26 个托尼类别中至少赢得一场胜利，这可能意味着保持开门与拉下帷幕之间的区别。
格莱美奖获得者 Pink 担任该节目的主持人，该节目将于周日晚上 8 点至 11 点在哥伦比亚广播公司 (CBS) 进行现场直播，并为美国东西海岸的 Paramount+ 订阅者进行流媒体直播。东部/下午 5 点至 8 点太平洋。
Pink 承诺会有一个宏大、轰鸣的开场曲——由 Benj Pasek、Justin Paul 和 Mark Sonnenblick 创作，最后舞台上约有 170 人——其中有大量的服装变化和一些钢丝工作，这是她在杂技音乐会上熟悉的。她聘请了《赛斯·迈耶斯深夜秀》的编剧兼表演者安布尔·鲁芬（Amber Ruffin）来帮忙讲笑话。
观众席上将有平克的母亲——她在费城长大时带她去看演出，灌输了对音乐剧的热爱——以及平克的两个孩子，音乐剧接力棒的传递。Pink 15 岁的女儿 Willow 是一位有抱负的戏剧演员，并敦促她主持托尼奖。
“她想让我说‘是’的最大原因是这样她就可以在节目中占有一席之地，因为她非常喜欢这个节目，”平克说。“我当时想，‘无论如何我也许可以给你找个座位。’”
七位最佳新音乐剧和最佳复兴音乐剧提名者将进行表演：《失落的男孩》、《施米加杜》、《泰坦尼克》、《两个陌生人（带着蛋糕穿越纽约）》、《猫：杰里科舞会》、《拉格泰姆》和《洛基恐怖秀》。
其他表演包括《摩门教之书》的原班主演——乔什·加德、安德鲁·兰内斯、罗里·奥马利和尼基·M·詹姆斯——今年庆祝该剧播出 15 周年。小莱斯利·奥多姆将在纪念部分演唱《吉屋出租》中的《没有你》，以纪念该剧播出 30 周年。
另一部庆祝里程碑的节目《芝加哥》现已迎来 30 周年，将有一个由 Pink 以及 Queen Latifah、Jesse Tyler Ferguson、Alex Newell、Adrienne Warren、Julianne Hough、Whitney Leavitt 和 Dylan Mulvaney 主演的表演时段。此外，去年庆祝了 50 周年的《A Chorus Line》将得到雷切尔·齐格勒 (Rachel Zegler) 的特别致敬。
最佳新音乐剧的竞争在四个截然不同的节目之间进行：《两个陌生人（带着蛋糕穿越纽约）》，一部异性吸引力的浪漫喜剧；《迷失的男孩》是根据 1987 年青少年电影吸血鬼惊悚片改编的舞台剧；“Schmigadoon！”温和地嘲讽了百老汇黄金时代的演出；以及改编自 1997 年电影《泰坦尼克号》的坎普音乐喜剧《泰坦尼克号》。
获得最佳戏剧提名的两部作品分别是《巨人》和《解放》，前者探讨了针对儿童作家罗尔德·达尔的反犹太主义指控，后者讲述了 20 世纪 70 年代一个提高意识的妇女团体，探讨不平等、性别角色和种族主义。
两个复兴类别中都有有趣的竞赛：内森·莱恩领衔的《推销员之死》正在与马克·斯特朗领衔的现代背景的《俄狄浦斯》和丹尼尔·雷德克里夫主演的甜蜜《一切辉煌》争夺最佳复兴剧。
最好的音乐复兴剧包括重新演绎为类似“姿势”的竞赛节目的新《猫》、席卷美国的历史节目《拉格泰姆》和欢快活泼的《洛基恐怖秀》。
比尔·劳赫 (Bill Rauch) 因联合导演重新构思的《猫：杰里克舞会》而首次获得托尼奖提名，在本季之前，他已连续三个赛季获得提名，并对百老汇目前的演出印象深刻。
“我以一名艺术家的身份看待这一季的一切，同时也以连续三年在百老汇看到丰富作品的人的身份来看待一切，”他说。“我只是认为百老汇有如此多的多样性，人们承担了如此多的艺术风险。我不得不说，我作为提名者的三年给我留下了深刻的印象。今年我也有这样的感觉。”
96 岁高龄的琼·斯奎布成为历史上最年长的托尼奖提名演员，如果她听到自己的名字，她可能会成为最年长的托尼奖得主，超越 2021 年获奖时 90 岁的洛伊丝·史密斯。莱恩希望凭借《推销员之死》第四次获得托尼奖，这将使他与博伊德·盖恩斯和弗兰克·兰格拉并列成为托尼奖历史上获奖最多的男演员。
有关 2026 年托尼奖的更多报道，请访问 https://apnews.com/hub/tony-awards。

item.title: 从未加满的油箱到更少的装饰，零售商看到美国消费者重新考虑他们的支出
item.description:
NEW YORK (AP) — U.S. consumers haven’t stopped spending money since the Iran wardrove upfuel prices, but many shoppers are reassessing what they buy and where, according to company executives and retail analysts.
The behavior changes observed so far are subtle, such as altered routines for buying gasoline and fewer visits to clothing and furniture stores. They also are uneven across the population. During recent earnings calls with analysts, executives from American mainstays like Walmart, McDonald’s and Dollar General cited overall shopper resilience as well as noticeable cutbacks bylower-income customers.
But the new signs of strain cited by major retailers as generous incometax refundshelped shore up their sales make some economists and analysts think they will see a wider retrenchment when the refunds are gone and consumers face the cumulative impact of more expensive gas and higher pricesfor food, clothing, insurance and other goods and services.
Trevor Chapman, a communications executive in West Hills, California, said that instead of going to a local independent gas station, he and his wife now plan their fuel stops around Costco stores with filling stations. The couple also is doing more online food shopping to avoid impulse buys, he said.
“Gas is a kind of catalyst,” Chapman said. “It trickles down into the entire budget. We’re trying to keep everything as normal as possible. But it’s starting to feel like it’s adding up more and more.”
Well before the U.S. and Israel launched the war, many consumers already were being more choosy with their discretionary purchases, fatigued by several years of stubborn inflation and tariffs on imported goods imposed last year.
The U.S. Commerce Department reported last week that higher prices, not more purchases, accounted for most of the growth in Americans’ spending in April, when akey inflation gaugereached the highest level since October 2023.
Members-only warehouse stores like Costco, Walmart’s Sam’s Club and BJ’s Wholesale Club have seen more traffic at their fuel pumps since the war began in late February, according to the companies. Fuel typically costs less at the wholesale clubs.
But many drivers are not filling their tanks up, Walmart Chief Financial Officer John David Rainey told analysts late last month. For the first time since 2022,Walmart customersand Sam’s Club members are buying an average of less than 10 gallons per trip, he said.
“That’s an indication of stress,” Rainey said.
Costco members also are making changes. They are visiting store gas stations more frequently to “top up in between what would have normally been a gap between getting the tank to empty because of the concern about what might the gas price be tomorrow,” Chief Financial Officer Gary Millerchip said in late May.
Meanwhile, the gas price surge has hurt convenience stores, where 80% of all fuel is sold in the U.S., according to Jeff Lenard, a vice president at the National Association of Convenience Stores.
A sales analysis by the trade group found that the number of pump transactions at the properties of 130convenience storecompanies fell by nearly 10% across March and April compared to the same two months last year. The number of sales inside the companies’ stores dropped by 10.4%, according to the analysis.
“When you lose gallons to the big box, you also lose in-store sales,” Lenard said.
Higher gas prices did not stop many Americans from dining out in the first two months of the war with Iran. Tax refunds helped, the National Restaurant Association said. Customer traffic at U.S. restaurants in April was unchanged from the same month last year, although a 2.6% increase in restaurant spending resulted largely from higher menu prices, according to market research firm Circana.
But cracks are starting to form as budget-conscious U.S. residents shoulder the combined weight of paying more for gas and other consumer goods on top of increasing costs in other areasfrom inflationpast and present.
The price of gas won’t help bring customers with household incomes of $45,000 or less back to U.S. fast-food restaurants, McDonald’s Chairman and CEOChris Kempczinski saidlast month. People in that income group began scaling back their fast-food purchases after the period of inflation that accompanied the end of the COVID-19 pandemic, and the trend picked up speed last year.
U.S.-based restaurant consulting firm Revenue Management Solutions analyzed 14.6 billion restaurant transactions from the last ‌four years ⁠and found that as gasoline gets more expensive, restaurant visits gradually decline, according to Chief Research Officer Sebastián Fernandez. The analysis indicated the impact doubles when gas hits the $4 mark, which it did asa nationwide averageon March 31.
Consumers also are making concessions when they shopfor groceries, according to Stew Leonard, president of an eight-store supermarket chain his father founded, Stew Leonard’s. He’s noticed customers buying meat in bulk to freeze and being less tempted to buy the products showcased during live food demonstrations or offered for sampling.
“It’s telling me that people are sticking more to their shopping list,” Leonard said.
Dollar General CEO Todd Vasos also cited$4 a gallongas as a tipping point that had more consumers with household incomes above $100,000 frequenting the discount chain. Vasos told analysts Tuesday that many of Dollar General’s core shoppers, who have mid-to-low incomes and live in rural areas, were paring back their food spending.
Sophie Tolsdorf, 29, of La Grange, Kentucky, said she is one of the consumers stocking up on meat when the price is reasonable. She also switched to buying whole fruit instead pre-cut fruit in containers and cut back on the rawhide bones for her dog that cost $40 a pack.
“He might have noticed,” Tolsdorf said. “He’s definitely a little bit bored during the workday now.”
Before the war, retailers had spent multiple earnings seasons highlighting consumer caution and selectivity as factors that could weigh on sales of nonessential products. Shoppers appear to havecurbed theirdiscretionary spending even more as the cost of buying gas went up, said Marshal Cohen, chief retail advisor at Circana.
Between April 25 and May 23, U.S. retailers sold 6% fewer non-grocery products than they did during the comparable four-week period of 2025, Cohen said. Housewares, clothing, footwear and sports equipment had the biggest declines, anywhere from 5% to 7%. Circana reported that toys and beauty items remained bright spots, registering at least an 8% increase in the number of units sold.
Location intelligence company Placer.ai, which tracks people’s movements based on cellphone usage, saw visits to the gas stations of BJ’s, Costco and Sam’s Club stores start to accelerate in early March, aligning with a sharp rise in fuel prices, according to R.J. Hottovy, the company’s head of analytical research.
By early May, Placer.ai’s data showed four consecutive weeks of reduced foot traffic at clothing, electronics and home furnishing stores, and more trips to grocery stores and dollar stores.
“Consumers are prioritizing value-oriented retailers like warehouse clubs, superstores, and off-price chains,” Hottovy said.
AP Food Writer Dee-Ann Durbin in Detroit contributed to the report.
